title: Mediterranean Pita-Style Flatbread

ingredients.name: wheat flour (or maida for softer pita)

subs.name: Half wheat flour and half maida gives a softer result; all maida makes a pita with a lighter texture that puffs more reliably
For a no-yeast quick version, use 1 teaspoon of baking powder instead; the bread will not puff into a pocket but will still be soft and flexible for wrapping
Any neutral oil or even a tablespoon of ghee added to the dough gives a slightly richer, more pliable pita

ingredients.name: warm water (around 40°C — comfortably warm to the touch)

steps.text: Dissolve the sugar and yeast in the warm water and let it stand for 5–8 minutes until it becomes foamy and slightly bubbly — this confirms the yeast is alive. If the mixture does not foam, the water was too hot or the yeast is expired; start again.

In a large bowl, combine the flour and salt. Make a well in the centre, add the yeast liquid and olive oil, and mix to a rough dough. Turn onto a clean surface and knead for 8–10 minutes until the dough is smooth, slightly tacky, and bounces back when you press it with a finger.

Form the dough into a ball, place in an oiled bowl, cover with a damp kitchen towel or cling film, and leave in a warm corner of the kitchen for 45–60 minutes until doubled in size. In an Indian kitchen this usually happens faster in the warmer months.

Punch down the risen dough and divide it into 6 equal portions. Roll each portion on a lightly floured surface into a thin circle about 20 cm across and 3–4 mm thick. Keep the thickness even for consistent puffing.

Heat a heavy tawa or skillet over high heat until it is very hot — a sprinkle of flour should turn light brown immediately. Place one pita on the dry tawa (no oil needed) and cook for 90 seconds to 2 minutes until bubbles appear on the surface and the underside has dark brown spots. Flip and cook for another 60–90 seconds. The pita should puff up at least partially — this is the characteristic pocket forming.

Remove the cooked pita and wrap immediately in a clean kitchen towel to keep it soft and flexible as it cools. Repeat with the remaining portions. Serve warm with tzatziki, hummus, or alongside any grilled dish. Pita keeps at room temperature for up to 24 hours; reheat briefly on a dry tawa for 30 seconds per side.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Fermentation conditions affect safety. Discard batches with unexpected mould, putrid odour or other spoilage signs.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: wheat-flour → Half wheat flour and half maida gives a softer result; all maida makes a pita with a lighter texture that puffs more reliably; yeast → For a no-yeast quick version, use 1 teaspoon of baking powder instead; the bread will not puff into a pocket but will still be soft and flexible for wrapping; olive-oil → Any neutral oil or even a tablespoon of ghee added to the dough gives a slightly richer, more pliable pita
indian_kitchen_adaptation: A heavy iron tawa or a thick non-stick tawa works superbly for this bread — the key is getting it genuinely very hot before the pita goes on. Indian wheat flour (atta) makes a slightly denser, healthier pita that is still delicious; maida gives the lightest, most pocket-forming result. The rising time is dramatically shorter in a warm Indian kitchen in summer (often 30–35 minutes is sufficient). The resulting pita is extremely similar to Indian chapati in technique and can be used interchangeably for wrapping souvlaki or serving with dips.
